title: If Nihilism is Murder, What Then? Camus' Distinctive Conception of Nihilism and Its Overcoming
author: Sharpe

opening_move.description: Opens by diagnosing Camus's persistent mischaracterization as a nihilist (due to Meursault, The Myth of Sisyphus's opening question about suicide). The paper argues this misreading misses how Camus from the start positioned nihilism as a problem to be overcome, not a condition to be embraced. The occasion is the renewed scholarly interest in nihilism (Jon Stewart's 2024 History of Nihilism) and the contemporary cultural-political climate.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Camus is a distinctive and undervalued thinker of nihilism whose post-1944 conception — nihilism as contempt for human life licensing deceit, enslavement, and murder — is a unique post-Nietzschean contribution irreducible to epistemological, reactionary, theological, or Nietzschean nihilism.

rivals_engaged: standard existentialist reading of Camus as nihilist
Novello's alignment of Camus with Nietzsche
four preceding conceptions of nihilism (epistemological, reactionary, theological, Nietzschean)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reconstructs Camus's distinctive nihilism concept in four parts: (1) maps four prior uses of 'nihilism' (Jacobi's epistemological, Russian-political, Christian-theological, Nietzschean); (2) reconstructs Camus's account of the absurd in early works, showing proximity and distance from prior nihilisms; (3) shows Camus's post-1944 definition (nihilism as contempt for human life licensing murder) as a new, historically-conditioned concept; (4) critiques Novello's alignment of Camus with Nietzsche, arguing Camus's 'measure' against Nietzsche's 'abyss' is a fundamental difference.

data_sources: Novello's Albert Camus as a Political Thinker (2010)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Intellectual history: genealogy of the term 'nihilism' from Jacobi through Nietzsche to 20th century; close reading of Camus's texts for his distinctive conception; comparative analysis between Camus's and Nietzsche's accounts; immanent critique of Novello's interpretation.
epistemic_mode.notes: This paper is purely_interpretive_hermeneutic because it makes no explicit normative claim about what follows for the present. The paper reconstructs Camus's historical-philosophical contribution to the nihilism debate and critiques a secondary reader. The contemporary relevance is gestured at in the opening (contemporary anomie, need for Camus's contribution) but not developed as a normative conclusion. The paper is closer to toribio_vazquez_2020 (genealogy of nihilism) and kastrinakis_2024 (Adorno-Nietzsche textual analysis) than to sharpe's potential alternative classification as normative.

normative_stake.domain: philosophy / nihilism / Camus / Nietzsche / critical theory of violence

key_concepts: nihilism as contempt for human life

engaged_critically: Freudian readings of Camus as nihilist

confidence_notes: Classified purely_interpretive_hermeneutic because the argument is entirely textual-genealogical: reconstructing Camus's distinctive conception of nihilism through close reading and intellectual history. No normative conclusion about what the present situation requires. The 'distinctive contribution' claim is descriptive-historical, not prescriptive. The contemporary climate reference in the opening is a motivation for the inquiry, not a normative application. Compare to toribio_vazquez_2020 (genealogy of nihilism) which has the same purely_interpretive_hermeneutic classification.
